one_line_takeaway: Membership-privacy leakage in classifiers concentrates in a small number of late-stage layers that contribute little to generalization, so freezing the early generalized layers and selectively retraining only those late risky layers with an existing MIA defense (PPTP) recovers more test accuracy and cuts training cost versus applying the same defense to the whole network from scratch.
key_finding: Disparity is regional, not global: on ResNet152/TinyImageNet the seen/unseen feature-map distance gap appears only starting in Stage 4 (3 of 33 blocks), with no disparity in Stages 1-3. Enlarging feature map size at the risky stage improves generalization without worsening disparity (ResNet18 test accuracy on TinyImageNet: 52.32% at 2x2, 54.58% at 4x4, 61.53% at 8x8, all with ~99.98% train accuracy), whereas increasing channel size worsens disparity while barely changing test accuracy (52.52% at 256 channels, 54.58% at 512, 53.99% at 1024, 55.03% at 2048). Ablation on TinyImageNet/ResNet18 (Figure 12) shows retraining only the last layer (FT-Last or WR-Last) fails to mitigate MIA success, while retraining multiple privacy-risky layers (WR-Multi) succeeds. On TinyImageNet with weight-freezing up to conv5_x (Stage 4) vs. CIFAR-100 freezing only up to conv4_x (Stage 3), PPTP combined with Adv-Reg and RelaxLoss shows larger utility gains than on CIFAR-100, where PPTP improves RelaxLoss and Adv-Reg's utility-privacy trade-off but does not significantly improve correctness-based MIA resistance specifically. PPTP also reduces training cost (Figure 16, CIFAR-100/ResNet18: lower CUDA memory and per-epoch time cost vs. training the full defense from scratch, attributed to needing under half the epochs to converge since fewer weights are retrained). RelaxLoss with PPTP achieves 49.59% test accuracy vs. 46.23% for RelaxLoss alone on TinyImageNet's AUC-ROC comparison (Figure 15), and Adv-Reg with PPTP reaches 37.58% vs. 34.12% for Adv-Reg alone, both at comparable-or-better MIA AUC.
core_contribution: The paper (Fang & Kim, NCSU) empirically shows that membership-privacy leakage in deep neural networks is not distributed uniformly across layers but concentrates in specific, identifiable regions (later stages/blocks) that are largely disjoint from the regions responsible for generalizable features. It introduces a sample-level feature-map-distance measurement method to locate where 'prediction disparity' (the seen-vs-unseen distributional gap that membership inference exploits) begins in a network, and proposes Privacy-Preserving Training Principle (PPTP): freeze the weights of layers identified as privacy-safe (already generalized) and only rewind-and-retrain the privacy-risky layers using an existing MIA defense, rather than retraining/defending the whole network from scratch.
threat_model: Membership inference attacks (MIAs) against trained classifier models: correctness-based MIA (Yeom et al. 2020), confidence-based MIA (Yeom et al. 2018; Song et al. 2019; Song & Mittal 2021), entropy-based MIA (Shokri et al. 2017; Song & Mittal 2021), modified-entropy-based MIA (Song & Mittal 2021), and neural-network-based MIA (Shokri et al. 2017; Nasr et al. 2018). The paper treats existing MIA defenses (Adv-Reg, SELENA, RelaxLoss) as pluggable components applied selectively to a subset of model layers rather than the whole model; it does not introduce a new attack, only a new way to apply existing defenses more surgically.
limitations: SELENA shows only slight improvement under PPTP on TinyImageNet and the paper attributes this partly to data augmentation being disabled during SELENA's knowledge-distillation phase (enabling it was reported as prohibitively costly, over 10x training time). SELENA results are omitted entirely on CIFAR-100 due to frequent gradient explosions during distillation (also observed less frequently on TinyImageNet). PPTP does not meaningfully improve resistance to correctness-based MIA on CIFAR-100 specifically, which the authors attribute to PPTP preserving CE-trained partially generalized features that do not benefit train/test correctness alignment. The paper's own scope is limited to image classification (CIFAR-100, TinyImageNet) with three architecture families and a single base architecture (ResNet18) for the main defense comparisons; it does not evaluate other data modalities, other MIA-adjacent attacks (model inversion, extraction), or larger-scale datasets/models. The identification of which layers are 'privacy-risky' (Table 6: conv4_x for CIFAR-100 vs conv5_x for TinyImageNet) is empirically determined per dataset/architecture rather than derived from a closed-form criterion, meaning the freezing boundary must be re-measured for new datasets or architectures.

quantitative_results: ResNet18/TinyImageNet feature-map-size sweep at Stage 4: test accuracy 52.32% (2x2), 54.58% (4x4), 61.53% (8x8); train accuracy 99.98% at all sizes
ATM-XT/TinyImageNet feature-map-size sweep: test accuracy 36.28% (1x) vs 47.29% (4x); train accuracy 99.97% vs 99.98%
ResNet18/TinyImageNet channel-size sweep at Stage 4: test accuracy 52.52% (256), 54.58% (512), 53.99% (1024), 55.03% (2048)
PPTP+RelaxLoss vs RelaxLoss alone on TinyImageNet (AUC-ROC figure): test accuracy 49.59% vs 46.23%
PPTP+Adv-Reg vs Adv-Reg alone on TinyImageNet (AUC-ROC figure): test accuracy 37.58% vs 34.12%
Cross-Entropy baseline on TinyImageNet: test accuracy 54.63%
Weight-freezing boundary (Table 6): conv4_x/Stage 3 for CIFAR-100 (ResNet18), conv5_x/Stage 4 for TinyImageNet (ResNet18)
CE Full vs CE Half vs RelaxLoss Full data comparison (Table 7, ResNet18/TinyImageNet): train/test accuracy 99.98%/54.58% (CE Full), 99.99%/44.07% (CE Half), 79.68%/47.57% (RelaxLoss Full)
Disparity in ResNet152/TinyImageNet begins abruptly at Stage 4, which comprises only 3 of the model's 33 total blocks
